title: What is a struct
instruction:
Imagine a library catalog card. One card holds a book's title, its author, its ISBN — all related, all together.

A struct is exactly that. It is a custom type that groups related fields under one name.

Run this. A `Book` struct is defined with two fields: `Title` and `Pages`. Then we create one and print it.
successMessage: One variable, two pieces of information. That is a struct. Notice Go prints it as {The Go Programming Language 400} — all fields together in curly braces.

title: Define a struct
instruction:
The syntax for defining a struct is:

```
type TypeName struct {
    FieldName FieldType
    FieldName FieldType
}
```

Field names start with a capital letter (so they are exported and accessible everywhere).

Define an `Author` struct with three fields: `Name` (string), `Nationality` (string), and `BirthYear` (int). Then create one and print it.
hint: type Author struct { Name string; Nationality string; BirthYear int }  — then a := Author{Name: "Margaret Atwood", Nationality: "Canadian", BirthYear: 1939} and fmt.Println(a)

codeChecks.message: Define a struct called Author: type Author struct { ... }
Add a Name string field to the struct.
Add a Nationality string field to the struct.
Add a BirthYear int field to the struct.
Create an Author value: a := Author{Name: ..., Nationality: ..., BirthYear: ...}
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your own custom type — defined, created, and printed. Every real Go application is full of types like this.

title: Access fields with dot notation
instruction:
To read a specific field, use a dot: `book.Title`, `book.Pages`.

You do not have to print the whole struct at once. Pull out exactly what you need.

Print `Title` and `Pages` on separate lines using dot notation.

codeChecks.message: Access the Title field: b.Title
Access the Pages field: b.Pages
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Dot notation is how you access every field in Go. Clean, readable, no guessing.

title: Update a field
instruction:
Fields work like variables. You can reassign them after creation.

`b.Pages = 350` — updates the Pages field directly.

Update `member.Active` to `true` and `member.FineBalance` to 0.0, then print the whole struct.

codeChecks.message: Update Active: member.Active = true
Update FineBalance: member.FineBalance = 0.0
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Same struct, updated fields. Notice the name stayed as Alice because we only changed Active and FineBalance.

title: Zero values in structs
instruction:
When you declare a struct without setting all fields, Go fills the rest with zero values — empty string for strings, 0 for ints, false for bools.

Run this and see what an unfilled struct looks like. You will recognise the zero values from the Variables chapter.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

type Book struct {
	Title   string
	Pages   int
	InStock bool
	Rating  float64
}

func main() {
	var b Book
	fmt.Println(b.Title)
	fmt.Println(b.Pages)
	fmt.Println(b.InStock)
	fmt.Println(b.Rating)
}
successMessage: Empty string, zero int, false bool, zero float. Every field gets its type's zero value. Safe and predictable — Go never leaves memory uninitialised.

title: Struct as a function parameter
instruction:
You can pass a struct into a function the same way you pass any value.

When you do, Go makes a copy. Changing the struct inside the function does not affect the original.

Call `printBook` with a book of your choice — your favourite title and page count.

codeChecks.message: Call printBook() with a Book value.
Create a Book struct with a Title field.
Replace the TODO comment with your call.

successMessage: Structs flow through functions just like ints and strings. Clean, readable, no globals required.

title: Struct as a return value
instruction:
Functions can return structs too.

This is how Go constructors work — a function that builds and returns a struct for you:

`func newBook(title string, pages int) Book { return Book{Title: title, Pages: pages} }`

Add a `newAuthor` function that takes a name and nationality and returns an `Author`. Call it and print the result.
hint: func newAuthor(name string, nationality string) Author { return Author{Name: name, Nationality: nationality} }

codeChecks.message: Define a newAuthor function that returns an Author.
Return an Author struct from newAuthor.
Replace the TODO comment with your function definition.

successMessage: Constructor pattern. One function, clean creation. This pattern is used everywhere in Go — especially in larger codebases.

title: Pointer to a struct
instruction:
When you pass a struct normally, Go copies it. Changes inside the function are lost.

Use a pointer to pass the original — not a copy:

`func addPages(b *Book) { b.Pages += 50 }`

Create a Book, call `addPages`, and see that Pages actually changed.
hint: b := &Book{Title: "Go in Action", Pages: 300}  addPages(b)  fmt.Println(b.Pages)

codeChecks.message: Create a pointer to a Book: b := &Book{...}
Call addPages(b) with the pointer.
Print b.Pages after calling addPages.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: The page count went up because addPages received a pointer — not a copy. The & creates a pointer, the * in the parameter says 'this is a pointer, modify the original'.

title: Nested structs
instruction:
A struct can contain another struct as a field.

This is how you model real-world relationships: a `Book` has an `Author`. A `Library` has `Books` and `Members`.

Run this. Then update `b.Author.Nationality` to "British" and print the full author.

codeChecks.message: Update b.Author.Nationality to "British"
Print the full b.Author.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Nested fields accessed with dot-dot notation. Real data is rarely flat — nested structs let you model it naturally.

title: Anonymous struct
successMessage: No type declaration needed. Anonymous structs are great for one-off groupings — shelf labels, config values, small response shapes. No name, no fuss.

title: Slice of structs
instruction:
In real programs you rarely have just one struct. You have a list of them — a slice of structs.

`books := []Book{{...}, {...}, {...}}`

Loop through the slice and print each book's title and page count.
hint: for _, b := range books { fmt.Printf("%s has %d pages\n", b.Title, b.Pages) }

codeChecks.message: Loop through books with range: for _, b := range books
Access b.Title inside the loop.
Access b.Pages inside the loop.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Slice of structs — the backbone of almost every Go application. Book lists, author catalogs, member directories. All of these are slices of structs.

title: Methods
instruction:
A method is a function attached to a struct. Instead of `printBook(b)` you write `b.Print()`. 

The receiver `(b Book)` before the function name links the method to the type:

```
func (b Book) Describe() {
    fmt.Println("Book:", b.Title, "-", b.Pages, "pages")
}
```

Add a `Summary()` method to `Member` that prints `"Name: X, Status: active/inactive"`. Then call it.
hint: func (m Member) Summary() { status := "inactive"; if m.Active { status = "active" }; fmt.Printf("Name: %s, Status: %s\n", m.Name, status) }

codeChecks.message: Add a method: func (m Member) Summary() { ... }
Call m.Summary() in main.
Replace the TODO comment with your method definition.

successMessage: Methods belong to a type. m.Summary() is cleaner than printMember(m). And it scales — add a dozen methods, everything stays organised.

title: Pointer receiver methods
instruction:
A method with a pointer receiver can modify the struct it is attached to.

`func (b *Book) AddPages(n int) { b.Pages += n }`

The `*` makes it a pointer receiver — changes to `b` inside the method affect the original.

Call `CheckOut` and print the `Active` status before and after.

codeChecks.message: Call member.CheckOut() on the struct.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: true before, false after. The pointer receiver method mutated the real struct, not a copy. Use pointer receivers whenever a method needs to change the struct.

title: Struct tags
instruction:
Struct tags are metadata attached to fields. They tell encoding libraries how to handle each field.

The most common use is JSON encoding:

```
type Book struct {
    Title string `json:"title"`
    Pages int    `json:"pages"`
}
```

The tag `json:"title"` tells Go's JSON encoder to use `title` (lowercase) in the JSON output instead of `Title`.

Run this and see the JSON output.
successMessage: The Go struct with capital letters became lowercase JSON keys. Struct tags control serialization — JSON, XML, database column names, form parsing. You'll use them in every real API.

title: Build a library catalog
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a library catalog from scratch:

1. Define an `Author` struct with fields: `Name` (string), `Nationality` (string)
2. Define a `Book` struct with fields: `Title` (string), `Pages` (int), `Author` (Author)
3. Add a `Print()` method to `Book` that prints all three fields formatted
4. Create a `[]Book` slice with at least 3 books
5. Loop through and call `.Print()` on each one

Output format for each book:
```
Title: Dune | Pages: 688 | Author: Frank Herbert (American)
```
hint: func (b Book) Print() { fmt.Printf("Title: %s | Pages: %d | Author: %s (%s)\n", b.Title, b.Pages, b.Author.Name, b.Author.Nationality) }

codeChecks.message: Define an Author struct.
Define a Book struct.
Add a Name string field to Author.
Add a Nationality string field to Author.
Add a Title string field to Book.
Add an Author field (of type Author) to Book.
Add a Print() method to Book.
Create a slice of Books: books := []Book{...}
Loop through books with range.
Call .Print() on each book.

successMessage: A complete library catalog. Custom types, nested struct, method, slice, loop — all the pieces working together. This is how Go programs are built in the real world. You are ready.
